God will have Israel released, Pharaoh opposes it, and the trial is whether God’s word or Pharaoh's will stand. The hand of the Lord immediately fell upon the cattle, many of them, some of all kinds, died from a disease. This caused great loss to their owners; they had made Israel poor, and now God would make them poor. The hand of God can be seen even in the sickness and death of cattle, for not even a sparrow falls to the ground without our Father’s knowledge. None of the Israelites' cattle should die; the Lord will separate them. The cattle died. The Egyptians worshipped their cattle. Whatever we idolize, it is right for God to remove from us. This proud tyrant and cruel oppressor deserved to be made an example by the just Judge of the universe. Those who are punished according to what they deserve cannot have any just cause to complain. A hard heart shows a state of mind upon which neither threats nor promises, neither judgments nor mercies, make any lasting impression. With their conscience numbed and their hearts filled with pride and presumption, they persist in unbelief and disobedience. This state of mind is also called a stony heart. Very different is the heart of flesh, the broken and contrite heart. Sinners have no one to blame but themselves for that pride and ungodliness which abuses God’s kindness and patience. For, however the Lord hardens the hearts of men, it is always as a punishment for former sins.

When the Egyptians were not moved by the death of their cattle, God sent a plague that seized their own bodies. If lesser judgments do not work, God will send greater ones. Sometimes God shows people their sin through their punishment. They had oppressed Israel in the furnaces, and now the ashes of the furnace are made terrifying to them. The plague itself was very severe. Even the magicians were struck with boils. Their power was restrained before; but they continued to resist Moses, and to strengthen Pharaoh’s unbelief, until they were forced to give way. Pharaoh remained stubborn. He had hardened his own heart, and now God justly gave him up to his own desires, allowing Satan to blind and harden him. If people shut their eyes against the light, it is right for God to close their eyes. This is the most severe judgment a person can be under outside of hell.

Moses is here ordered to deliver a dreadful message to Pharaoh. Providence arranged that Moses should deal with a man of such a fierce and stubborn spirit as this Pharaoh; and everything made it a clear example of God’s power to humble and bring down the proudest of his enemies. When God's justice threatens ruin, his mercy at the same time shows a way of escape from it. God not only distinguished between Egyptians and Israelites, but also between some Egyptians and others. If Pharaoh will not yield and prevent the judgment itself, those who take warning may find shelter. Some believed what was spoken, and they feared, and brought in their servants and cattle; and that was wise. Even among Pharaoh’s servants, some trembled at God's word; should not the sons of Israel dread it? But others did not believe, and left their cattle in the field. Stubborn unbelief is deaf to the fairest warnings and wisest counsel, leaving the blood of those who perish on their own heads.

This hail caused terrible destruction: it killed both people and livestock; the corn above ground was destroyed, and only what had not yet sprouted was preserved. The land of Goshen was protected. God causes rain or hail on one city and not another, either in mercy or judgment. Pharaoh humbled himself to Moses. No one could have spoken better: he admits he was wrong; he acknowledges that the Lord is righteous; and God must be justified when he speaks, even if it is with thunder and lightning. Yet his heart remained hardened all this time. Moses pleaded with God; though he had reason to think Pharaoh would repent of his repentance, he told him so, yet he promised to be his friend. Moses went out of the city, despite the hail and lightning that kept Pharaoh and his servants indoors. Peace with God makes people immune to thunder. Pharaoh was frightened by the tremendous judgment; but when it was over, his kind promises were forgotten. Those who are not improved by judgments and mercies commonly become worse.
